channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump’s Awful Week
item.description: The U.S. president is picking fights in every direction, alienating key supporters and allies.

item.title: Peru’s Political Thriller
item.description: The country has had nine presidents in the last decade. Who will be next?

item.title: Pope Leo Blasts ‘Tyrants’ in Thinly Veiled Critique of Trump
item.description: The White House has lashed out at the Vatican’s latest sermons on war and peace.

item.title: Republicans Twiddle Their Thumbs on Iran as Democrats Seethe
item.description: Trump’s party is still providing cover on unauthorized war despite growing signs of misgivings.

item.title: 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Iran
item.description: In the war’s aftermath, key details about the regime remain opaque.

item.title: Ukraine’s Success Still Needs Troops More Than Robots
item.description: Kyiv is struggling with skepticism of conscription fueled by Russian propaganda.

item.title: Why eldest siblings are brainier
item.description: A new study finds that sickness may play a role

item.title: The Man Who Represents Post-Clerical Iran
item.description: The rise of Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr signals the consolidation of a new type of state.

item.title: Can the Arctic Council Survive?
item.description: With the rift over Greenland, the intergovernmental body finds itself in troubled waters again.

item.title: Trump Has Made the Case for International Law
item.description: By taking a wrecking ball to the global rules-based order, Donald Trump has thrown the entire world into chaos, reminding us why our post-World War II forebears laid the foundation for that order. The task now is to re-imagine an international legal regime that works more reliably for more people, without exception for the powerful.

item.title: Adam Smith on the US Midterms
item.description: The Wealth of Nations offers a useful lens for understanding why US President Donald Trump’s mercantilist agenda has fallen short of its own stated goals. It also points to a better path, combining competitive markets with policies that help workers and communities build skills and keep pace with economic change.

item.title: When Climate Lies Kill: Red-Tagging Indigenous Defenders in the Philippines
item.description: Climate disinformation is strategically deployed to reinforce red-tagging narratives, portraying Indigenous resistance to mining, energy, and infrastructure projects as a threat to national security.

item.title: Tumour cells use a genetic trick to become drug-resistant
item.description: The same trick could also be used against them

item.title: How to Future-Proof the Global Economy
item.description: The backlash against globalization, the slow death of the Washington Consensus, and the rapid rise of AI are fueling volatility that, if left unchecked, will lead to lower growth, higher inflation, and greater inequality. To move the economy onto a better path requires, first and foremost, abandoning our faith in outdated ideas.

item.title: Beyond the Rupture: Where Are China-Japan Relations Heading?
item.description: The evolution of Sino-Japanese relations has rarely followed a linear path – but it has always remained within clear guardrails, even during times of tension.

item.title: 5-Year Prison Terms for Atajurt Activists Who Burned Chinese Flag
item.description: After protesting Chinese state policies in Xinjiang, and after apparent pressure from Beijing, the group of Kazakhs were charged with “inciting national hatred.”

item.title: Australia’s New National Defense Strategy Feels Written for a Bygone Era
item.description: It ignores the elephant in the room: an unreliable and unruly United States.

item.title: China’s Taiwan Calculus Ahead of the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: Beijing is not seeking a breakthrough on Taiwan at the Trump-Xi summit, but rather incremental gains that could gradually weaken Taiwan-U.S. ties.

item.title: The impending global food shock is preventable
item.description: So why won’t it be prevented?

item.title: Donald Trump has made Venezuela a better place
item.description: But the American president is misinterpreting an incomplete success

item.title: Peter Magyar’s victory over Viktor Orban will keep Hungary in the spotlight
item.description: The country will become a test case for reversing democratic decay

item.title: Soaring numbers of Chinese women demand divorce
item.description: They are defying state efforts to make it harder

item.title: Training for Beijing’s humanoid half-marathon is gruelling
item.description: China is racing towards a robotic future

item.title: J.D. Vance’s theory of Trumpism is no match for the practice
item.description: This White House is a graveyard for grand strategies

item.title: POTUS v the Pope
item.description: Running short of new people to poke, the president has a go at God’s vicar

item.title: The big business of survival bunkers
item.description: Another sign that prepping has gone mainstream

item.title: The Hudson is now so clean that everyone can eat from it
item.description: Battery sashimi, anyone?

item.title: Venezuela is not the triumph Donald Trump claims, but it’s improving
item.description: Economic growth may accelerate and the regime has become less repressive

item.title: Peter Magyar’s victory will keep Hungary in the spotlight
item.description: The country will become a test case for reversing democratic decay

item.title: Singapore and Malaysia knock heads over the war in Iran
item.description: They have their own strait to worry about

item.title: The game theory behind violating ceasefires
item.description: Truces like the one in the Gulf get broken all the time. But they still work

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Politics
Business
European policymakers fiddle with energy prices, again.
item.description: By subsidising fuel, they are delaying adjustments in demand

item.title: Where should Pablo Picasso’s “Guernica” be allowed to hang?
item.description: Arguments over the painting’s rightful place hark back to ugly regional politics

item.title: A scramble ahead of France’s presidential election
item.description: A populist-right victory is possible, but not inevitable

item.title: “Complete change of regime” in Hungary
item.description: Peter Magyar vows to root out corruption

item.title: The desperate pursuit of final approval
item.description: An office parable

item.title: Australia’s startup scene is thriving at last
item.description: Can the Lucky Country recreate Silicon Valley down under?

item.title: A landslide victory in Benin’s presidential election was hardly democratic
item.description: One of west Africa’s bastions of democracy has fallen

item.title: Is bombing power plants and oil facilities a war crime?
item.description: The answer is more complicated than it seems

item.title: The Gulf war has settled into an uneasy limbo
item.description: Fighting is paused, Hormuz is shut and prospects for a deal are uncertain

item.title: Rio de Janeiro is a beautiful warning to the rest of Brazil
item.description: Local politicians are too often corrupt and criminal

item.title: A victory in Benin’s presidential election was hardly democratic
item.description: One of west Africa’s bastions of democracy has fallen

item.title: Is the US Falling Victim to the Resource Curse?
item.description: The argument that the natural-resource curse is at work in the US rests on the assumption that rising oil and gas production will necessarily crowd out manufacturing, including renewables. But while the argument is increasingly heard, past experience in the US—as well as in Australia, Chile, Norway, and elsewhere—does not support it.

item.title: PPP’s Chief Holds Meetings in Washington While His Party Faces Ruin at Home
item.description: People Power Party leader Jang Dong-hyeok’s Washington trip reveals more about his internal survival strategy than any genuine foreign policy agenda.

item.title: China’s Definition of an ‘Evil Cult’ Is Expanding Beyond Religious Groups
item.description: “Create Abundance” is not, in any conventional sense, a religion. For that reason, its persecution signals a dangerous expansion of China’s ideological control.

item.title: A New Dataset Maps Central Asia’s Extractive Economy
item.description: An open-access dataset from the Oxus Society maps more than $118 billion in resource exports across the five Central Asian republics, offering a rare quantitative window into the region’s shifting place in Eurasian supply chains.

item.title: Only Peace Can Make a New World Order
item.description: Today’s wars are tearing down the existing global system, but they can’t replace it.

item.title: Is the Iran War America’s Suez or Its Gallipoli?
item.description: The Iran war is not America’s "Suez moment," but it may well turn out to be America’s Gallipoli moment. It was there, in 1915, that the British Empire overreached disastrously—underestimating the tenacity of a decadent regime, overestimating its own military capacity, and demonstrating stupendous strategic confusion.

item.title: Hungary’s Counter-Counterrevolution
item.description: Ordinary Hungarians have paid a steep price for Viktor Orbán’s smash-and-grab mafia state, where family and friends enriched themselves while living standards stagnated. Voters have turned the page on Orbán, and now the question is whether Hungary can turn the corner after 16 years of his "illiberal democracy."

item.title: Xinjiang’s Repression of Uyghurs Has Evolved, Not Ended
item.description: A rare insider testimony reveals how China tries to hide state violence in Xinjiang

item.title: Collapsing remittances will compound Asia’s energy shock
item.description: Could workers kicked out of the Gulf find fortune in East Asia?

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The ugly history of “America First”
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, revisits a moment of fierce isolationist sentiment

item.title: Could AI’s leading men become as powerful as Ford or Rockefeller?
item.description: For now, they are still a long way behind

item.title: Millions will go hungry if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed
item.description: Expensive fuel and fertiliser will lead to smaller harvests and higher prices

item.title: India’s space industry is blasting off
item.description: Thank Nehru, Modi and Musk

item.title: Nepal’s New PM Balendra Shah Faces First Diplomatic Test With Upcoming India Visit
item.description: In New Delhi, he will not want to be seen to be too close to “big brother” India so early in his tenure.

item.title: Introducing our Asia Bulletin newsletter
item.description: Every week, our team of correspondents will write about topics as varied as the region itself

item.title: If Awami League Can Be Banned, Why Not the Jamaat on the Same Charges?
item.description: The Jamaat-e-Islami was complicit in the genocide of 3 million people in 1971. Why is that not reason enough for the BNP government to ban it?

item.title: Global imbalances are back. Who’s to blame?
item.description: The suspects look familiar

item.title: Pakistan’s deft diplomacy is an economic blessing. And a curse.
item.description: It allows the country’s rulers to put off necessary reforms

item.title: America will come to regret its war on taxes
item.description: Lately, Democrats have joined the charge

item.title: Sri Lanka’s Fertility Fall May Outlast Its Economic Recovery
item.description: The country will need to prepare for the consequences of sustained low fertility rates.

item.title: Why China’s government worries about AI
item.description: Its concerns include jobs, security and beating America

item.title: America wakes up to AI’s dangerous power
item.description: After Mythos, a laissez-faire approach is no longer politically tenable or strategically wise

item.title: Cyberwar’s New Frontier
item.description: How AI agents will threaten global security.

item.title: What the Iran War Means for the “Axis of Resistance”
item.description: The Iran war is a crucible for Shiite identity and politics.

item.title: AI Moves Fast. Southeast Asia’s Cybersecurity Policy Doesn’t.
item.description: Anthropic’s Mythos Preview exposes a widening gap between accelerating AI-enabled threats and Southeast Asia’s capacity to respond — and the window to act is closing.

item.title: How China Is Positioning Itself Ahead of the Trump–Xi Summit
item.description: Beijing is working subtly to shape the regional environment ahead of the meeting – and take advantage of Washington’s focus on the Iran war.
